past_medical_history: Right temporal lobe epilepsy diagnosed at age 28, secondary to right hippocampal sclerosis from childhood febrile seizures
Childhood febrile seizures (ages 1-4, multiple prolonged episodes)
Seizure-free for 3 years on lamotrigine monotherapy (last confirmed epileptic seizure 3 years ago)
Generalized anxiety disorder diagnosed at age 30
History of sexual abuse in childhood (disclosed during prior neuropsychological evaluation at age 32)

medications.frequency: As needed (taking 2-3 times daily over past 2 months, increased from prior PRN use)

family_history: Daughter (age 16) newly diagnosed with epilepsy 3 months ago
Father with hypertension and type 2 diabetes
No other family history of epilepsy

social_history.summary: Dental hygienist, working full-time but has missed significant work due to 'seizures' over the past 2 months. Married 16 years. Two children (ages 16 and 13). Daughter was diagnosed with epilepsy 3 months ago, causing the patient significant guilt and anxiety ('I gave her this disease'). Non-smoker. No alcohol (avoids due to epilepsy). No illicit drug use. Increasing alprazolam use over past 2 months.

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented x4. GCS 15. Cooperative. Mild anxiety on interview. Tearful when discussing daughter's epilepsy diagnosis. MMSE 30/30. No focal cognitive deficits.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II-XII intact. No visual field deficits. Pupils equal and reactive.
neurological_exam.motor: 5/5 strength throughout all extremities. Normal muscle bulk and tone.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric throughout. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Normal finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin bilaterally. No dysmetria.
neurological_exam.special_signs: No events witnessed during initial examination. Normal interictal neurological exam (as expected with well-controlled temporal lobe epilepsy).

patient.chief_complaint: My seizures are back and worse than ever
patient.history_present_illness: 38-year-old right-handed Hispanic female with a 10-year history of well-controlled right temporal lobe epilepsy (hippocampal sclerosis, on lamotrigine 200 mg BID, seizure-free for 3 years) presenting with a 2-month history of increasing 'seizure' frequency, now 2-3 events per week. She has been to the emergency department 4 times in the past 2 months for these events. However, the semiology of the new events differs significantly from her prior documented epileptic seizures: (1) longer duration — the new events last 5-20 minutes (prior seizures lasted 1-2 minutes), (2) eyes closed during events (prior seizures had eyes open with staring), (3) wild bilateral thrashing with side-to-side head movement (prior seizures had behavioral arrest with oral automatisms — lip smacking and chewing), (4) preserved awareness during some events — she reports remembering everything during the episode, (5) no post-ictal confusion (prior seizures always had 20-30 minutes of post-ictal confusion and word-finding difficulty). The precipitant: 3 months ago, her 16-year-old daughter was diagnosed with epilepsy, causing severe maternal guilt and anxiety. She has been taking increasing amounts of alprazolam.

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Right hippocampal FLAIR hyperintensity, consistent with sclerosis
signal_characteristics.T1: Right hippocampal volume loss compared to left

volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 2.4 cm3 (reduced, 5th percentile — STABLE from prior MRI 2 years ago when it was 2.5 cm3)
volumetrics.asymmetry_index: Significant right < left asymmetry (consistent with right hippocampal sclerosis)

additional_observations: Right hippocampal sclerosis is STABLE compared to prior MRI from 2 years ago
No new mass, enhancement, or signal abnormality
No dual pathology on imaging (no cortical dysplasia, no tumor)
mri.impression: Known right hippocampal sclerosis, stable compared to prior imaging. No new lesions, mass, or enhancement. No imaging explanation for increased seizure frequency.
recommended_actions: Clinical correlation — stable imaging does not explain increased event frequency
Video-EEG monitoring recommended to characterize the new events

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-organized, reactive

findings.morphology: Moderate amplitude sharp waves with after-going slow wave, phase reversal at F8
findings.clinical_correlation: Known interictal epileptiform discharges from the right temporal focus, consistent with the patient's established epilepsy diagnosis. This is a KNOWN FINDING, unchanged from prior EEGs.

activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving, no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Abnormal routine EEG due to occasional right anterior temporal sharp waves — a KNOWN finding from prior EEGs, unchanged. No seizures captured during this 30-minute routine recording. Background activity is normal.
eeg.limitations: 30-minute routine recording; no clinical events captured. Prolonged video-EEG monitoring recommended to capture habitual events.
recommended_actions: Prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture and characterize the patient's current events
Check lamotrigine level to confirm therapeutic dosing

AED_Levels.clinical_significance: Lamotrigine level is therapeutic, confirming adequate dosing and compliance. The increased event frequency is NOT due to subtherapeutic AED levels.

Other.clinical_significance: NORMAL post-event prolactin. Epileptic generalized tonic-clonic seizures typically cause a 2-3x rise in prolactin within 20 minutes. A normal post-event prolactin argues against an epileptic seizure, particularly a GTC, and is consistent with PNES.

labs.interpretation: Normal labs including CBC, BMP, and therapeutic lamotrigine level (8.2 mcg/mL). Therapeutic AED level confirms compliance and adequate dosing — the increased events are not due to subtherapeutic lamotrigine. Post-event prolactin drawn 30 minutes after an ED event is NORMAL (11.2 ng/mL), which argues against an epileptic seizure (particularly GTC) and is consistent with PNES.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-organized, reactive throughout 5-day monitoring
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture maintained throughout monitoring period

findings.type: Interictal right anterior temporal sharp waves — KNOWN, STABLE
findings.frequency: Occasional (2-5 per hour, predominantly in drowsiness and sleep)
findings.morphology: Moderate amplitude sharp waves with after-going slow wave, phase reversal at F8
findings.clinical_correlation: Known interictal epileptiform discharges consistent with the underlying right temporal lobe epilepsy. Unchanged from comparison EEGs from 2 and 4 years ago.

findings.location: No EEG correlate — normal background throughout
findings.morphology: Video: patient reported feeling anxious, then developed bilateral arm and leg thrashing, side-to-side head movement, eyes CLOSED, back arching. EEG showed preserved normal alpha rhythm throughout the entire event with NO epileptiform activity, no ictal rhythm, and no post-ictal slowing.
findings.clinical_correlation: Clinical event WITHOUT electrographic correlate = psychogenic non-epileptic seizure (PNES). Semiology: eyes closed, bilateral thrashing, preserved background rhythm.

findings.morphology: Video: triggered by phone call from daughter's neurologist. Patient became tearful, then developed bilateral arm shaking with waxing-and-waning intensity, eyes closed, pelvic thrusting. Nurse noted patient responded to voice command during event ('squeeze my hand') then resumed shaking. EEG completely normal throughout.
findings.clinical_correlation: PNES. Identifiable emotional trigger, responsiveness during event, waxing-waning pattern.

findings.type: Clinical Events 3-7 (Days 2-5) — ALL without electrographic correlate
findings.location: No EEG correlate for any event
findings.frequency: 5 additional events captured, lasting 5-15 minutes each
findings.morphology: All events showed similar semiology: eyes closed, bilateral thrashing, side-to-side head movement, variable duration. EEG remained normal throughout ALL events. No post-ictal slowing after any event.
findings.clinical_correlation: All 7 captured events are PNES. Zero electrographic seizures captured during 5-day monitoring.

artifacts.impact: Obscured some channels during vigorous movements but underlying alpha rhythm still identifiable on referential montages

output.impression: 5-day continuous video-EEG monitoring captured 7 clinical events: ALL 7 events showed clinical semiology (eyes closed, bilateral thrashing, side-to-side head movement, lasting 5-15 minutes) WITHOUT any electrographic ictal correlate. EEG remained normal during all events. Diagnosis: psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES). Interictal EEG showed the known right temporal sharp waves, STABLE and unchanged from prior EEGs. No electrographic seizures captured. Lamotrigine level during admission: 9.1 mcg/mL (therapeutic). The underlying epilepsy is stable; the new events are PNES.
output.limitations: No electrographic seizures were captured, which is consistent with 3 years of seizure freedom on lamotrigine. The interictal sharp waves confirm the underlying epilepsy remains present but controlled.
recommended_actions: Deliver dual diagnosis: underlying epilepsy (controlled) + new PNES
Do NOT increase lamotrigine for PNES events
Refer for psychological treatment for PNES

Prolactin_Testing.test: Post-event prolactin (drawn 18 minutes after captured PNES event)
Prolactin_Testing.clinical_significance: No post-ictal prolactin elevation. An epileptic generalized tonic-clonic seizure typically causes a 2-3x rise in prolactin within 10-20 minutes. The absence of prolactin elevation is consistent with PNES. NOTE: prolactin is less reliable for focal seizures without secondary generalization.
Ratio of 1.05 (essentially no change) is consistent with PNES, not epileptic seizure

output.interpretation: Post-event prolactin (12.4 ng/mL) drawn 18 minutes after a captured PNES event shows no elevation from baseline (11.8 ng/mL). The post-event/baseline ratio of 1.05 is far below the >2.0 ratio expected after an epileptic GTC seizure. This supports the video-EEG finding that the captured events are PNES, not epileptic seizures.

Psychiatric_Assessment.clinical_significance: Moderate PTSD related to childhood sexual abuse. Intrusive memories have intensified since daughter reached the age at which patient's abuse began.
Moderate depression with prominent guilt, sleep disturbance, and fatigue
Severe anxiety focused on daughter's health, fear of daughter having seizures, fear of being a 'bad mother'

Psychiatric_Assessment.value: Daughter's epilepsy diagnosis 3 months ago is the primary precipitant. Patient experiences overwhelming guilt ('I gave her this disease'), catastrophic fear about daughter's future, and re-activation of her own traumatic memories. Reports that PNES events often occur when thinking about or discussing daughter's condition.
Psychiatric_Assessment.clinical_significance: Clear identifiable psychological precipitant for PNES onset

Psychiatric_Assessment.value: Escalating from 0.5 mg PRN (1-2x/week) to 0.5 mg 2-3x daily over 2 months. Reports needing it to 'calm the shaking.' Difficulty going without it for more than 12 hours.
Psychiatric_Assessment.clinical_significance: Concerning pattern of benzodiazepine dependence development. Interdose withdrawal may be contributing to event frequency.

Psychiatric_Assessment.value: Sexual abuse ages 8-12 by family friend. Previously disclosed during neuropsychological evaluation. Has not completed trauma-focused therapy.
Psychiatric_Assessment.clinical_significance: Known risk factor for PNES. Daughter reaching age of patient's abuse may have triggered re-traumatization.

output.interpretation: Psychiatric evaluation reveals moderate PTSD (childhood sexual abuse, reactivated), moderate depression, and severe anxiety. The daughter's epilepsy diagnosis 3 months ago is a clear precipitant for PNES onset, triggering overwhelming guilt, catastrophizing, and reactivation of traumatic memories. History of childhood sexual abuse is a well-established risk factor for PNES. Concerning pattern of escalating alprazolam use suggesting developing benzodiazepine dependence. Recommended: trauma-focused CBT for PNES, gradual alprazolam taper, and continued psychiatric follow-up.
abnormal_values_summary: Escalating alprazolam use concerning for dependence

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: Comparison of current interictal EEG with prior EEGs from 2 and 4 years ago

findings.type: Right anterior temporal sharp waves — UNCHANGED across all 3 recordings
findings.frequency: Current: 2-5/hour. 2 years ago: 3-5/hour. 4 years ago: 2-4/hour. No significant change in interictal burden.
findings.morphology: Identical morphology across all recordings: moderate amplitude with after-going slow wave, phase reversal at F8
findings.state: All predominantly during drowsiness and light sleep
findings.clinical_correlation: The interictal epileptiform discharge burden is STABLE over the past 4 years, suggesting the underlying epilepsy is not worsening. The increased clinical events represent a NEW phenomenon (PNES) rather than progression of the epilepsy.

output.impression: Comparison of current interictal EEG with prior recordings from 2 and 4 years ago demonstrates identical right anterior temporal sharp waves with stable frequency and morphology. No change in interictal epileptiform discharge burden. The underlying epilepsy appears stable, supporting the conclusion that the new clinical events are PNES rather than breakthrough epileptic seizures.
recommended_actions: Stable interictal findings support maintaining current lamotrigine dose
New events are PNES — AED adjustment is not indicated

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) coexisting with controlled right temporal lobe epilepsy

differential.key_distinguishing: Therapeutic lamotrigine level (8.2-9.1 mcg/mL), normal ictal EEG during all 7 captured events, semiology completely different from prior documented epileptic seizures (eyes closed vs open, thrashing vs behavioral arrest, 5-20 min vs 1-2 min, no post-ictal confusion), normal post-ictal prolactin.

MRI stable with no new lesions. Interictal EEG unchanged from prior recordings. Video-EEG captured 7 events with NO electrographic ictal correlate — the gold standard for distinguishing PNES from epileptic seizures.
Lamotrigine levels consistently therapeutic (8.2 on admission, 9.1 during monitoring), confirming regular medication intake.
No new medications that affect lamotrigine metabolism have been added. Lamotrigine levels are therapeutic and stable.

optimal_actions.action: Admit for prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture and characterize the current events with simultaneous EEG recording
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Events captured WITHOUT electrographic correlate, confirming PNES

optimal_actions.action: Compare current event semiology with the patient's prior documented epileptic seizure semiology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Completely different semiology: eyes closed vs open, thrashing vs behavioral arrest, long vs short, no post-ictal confusion vs 20-30 min confusion

optimal_actions.action: Check lamotrigine level to confirm therapeutic dosing and exclude non-compliance
Obtain MRI to rule out new structural pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Stable hippocampal sclerosis, no new lesions

optimal_actions.action: Deliver the dual diagnosis clearly: 'Your epilepsy is well controlled on your medication, but you have developed a new type of episode that is different from your seizures. These new episodes are called non-epileptic seizures.'
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient understanding of the dual diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Do NOT increase lamotrigine or add additional AEDs for PNES events
Refer for trauma-focused psychotherapy (CBT for PNES)
Address escalating alprazolam use and develop a safe taper plan to prevent benzodiazepine dependence

critical_actions: Perform video-EEG monitoring to capture events and correlate with EEG
Distinguish PNES semiology from the patient's known epileptic seizure semiology
Check AED levels to confirm therapeutic dosing
Deliver the dual diagnosis: 'Your epilepsy is well controlled, but you have developed a new type of episode that is different'
DO NOT increase AEDs for PNES
Refer for trauma-focused psychotherapy (CBT for PNES)
Address alprazolam use and potential benzodiazepine dependence
contraindicated_actions: Increasing lamotrigine dose for events that are PNES, not epileptic
Administering IV benzodiazepines for prolonged PNES events (risks respiratory depression without benefit)
Discontinuing lamotrigine (the underlying epilepsy still needs treatment)
Telling the patient her events are 'fake' or 'not real'
key_reasoning_points: PNES coexists with epilepsy in 10-20% of cases — having epilepsy does NOT exclude PNES

Key semiology differences: eyes closed (PNES) vs eyes open (epileptic), bilateral thrashing vs behavioral arrest/automatisms, long duration (5-20 min) vs short (1-2 min), preserved awareness vs impaired awareness, no post-ictal confusion vs 20-30 min confusion
Normal ictal EEG during clinical events is the gold standard for PNES diagnosis
Normal post-ictal prolactin supports PNES (epileptic GTC seizures typically elevate prolactin 2-3x)
Therapeutic AED levels exclude non-compliance as the cause of 'increased seizures'
Stable interictal EEG and MRI confirm the underlying epilepsy is unchanged and controlled
Psychological precipitant (daughter's epilepsy diagnosis) is identifiable and treatable
Prior childhood sexual abuse is a well-established risk factor for PNES

red_herrings.intended_effect: Every new event in an epilepsy patient is reflexively attributed to epilepsy, leading to AED escalation
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Having epilepsy does not make all events epileptic. PNES coexists with epilepsy in 10-20% of patients. Video-EEG is essential to distinguish.

red_herrings.data_point: Right temporal sharp waves on routine EEG
red_herrings.intended_effect: Confirms active epilepsy and seems to explain the current events
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Interictal sharp waves confirm the UNDERLYING epilepsy but do not prove the CURRENT events are epileptic. The sharp waves are stable and unchanged from prior EEGs. Ictal EEG during events is needed to determine whether the events are epileptic.

red_herrings.data_point: Family history of epilepsy in daughter
red_herrings.intended_effect: Suggests genetic predisposition and possible worsening of maternal epilepsy
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: The daughter's diagnosis is the psychological TRIGGER for PNES development — it caused overwhelming maternal guilt and anxiety. It is not evidence of worsening maternal epilepsy.

metadata.diagnostic_challenge: Dual pathology: PNES coexisting with controlled temporal lobe epilepsy. The known epilepsy creates a strong cognitive anchor that all events are epileptic. Requires video-EEG to distinguish PNES from epileptic seizures, careful semiology comparison, and recognition that AED escalation is harmful for PNES.
